name: TypeScript Strict Mode
description: Strict TypeScript rules for maximum type safety: no any, proper generics, discriminated unions, exhaustive matching, and Result types
author: StartUpp AI IDE
rules: Never use 'any' type -- use 'unknown' when the type is truly unknown, then narrow with type guards
Enable all strict compiler options: strict, noUncheckedIndexedAccess, exactOptionalPropertyTypes, noImplicitReturns
Use discriminated unions with a literal 'type' or 'kind' field for modeling variants, not optional fields or type unions without discriminants
Implement exhaustive pattern matching with 'never' in switch default cases to catch unhandled variants at compile time
Prefer Result<T, E> types (e.g., { ok: true, data: T } | { ok: false, error: E }) over throwing exceptions for expected error paths
Use 'satisfies' operator to validate object literals against types while preserving the narrowest inferred type
Define function return types explicitly for public APIs and module exports; inferred types are fine for local helpers
Use branded types (nominal typing via intersection with { readonly __brand: unique symbol }) for domain IDs to prevent mixing UserId with OrderId
Prefer readonly arrays and readonly properties by default; only make mutable when mutation is necessary
Use template literal types and mapped types to derive related types from a single source of truth instead of manually keeping types in sync

promptTemplates.name: Type-Safe Refactor
promptTemplates.description: Refactor code to be strictly typed

promptTemplates.name: Create Type-Safe API Client
promptTemplates.description: Generate a fully typed API client
promptTemplates.template: Create a type-safe API client for the following endpoints: {{endpoints}}. Use branded types for IDs, Result types for responses, and ensure all error cases are explicitly typed. Include request/response type definitions.

conventions:
TypeScript conventions:
- Use .ts/.tsx extensions; never .js in a TypeScript project
- Shared types go in src/types/ directory
- Export types from index.ts barrel files per module
- Use 'interface' for object shapes that may be extended; use 'type' for unions, intersections, and computed types
- Avoid enums; prefer const objects with 'as const' assertion and derive types from them
- Place type guards in a utils/typeGuards.ts file
- Run tsc --noEmit in CI to catch type errors without producing output
